article.title: Capital, Control, and Commodification: Structuring Tradable and Non-Tradable Rights in a California Subchapter T Cooperative

title.emphasis: Introduction: The Modern Cooperative's Dilemma

section.para: The cooperative business model, founded on principles of democratic member control and mutual benefit, presents a profound and persistent challenge in the modern economy: the cooperative capital conundrum.1 While investor-owned corporations have well-established pathways to capital markets, cooperatives are structurally constrained by their foundational tenets. The very principles that define them—such as one-member, one-vote governance and the subordination of capital to the service of patronage—are often antithetical to the expectations of traditional equity investors seeking returns proportional to their capital and control commensurate with their investment. This inherent tension creates significant hurdles for cooperatives seeking to raise the capital necessary for growth, innovation, and long-term sustainability.

This report addresses this central challenge by providing a rigorous analytical framework for structuring ownership and management rights within a cooperative formed under California law and operating under the federal tax provisions of Subchapter T. The core purpose is to explore the boundaries of the cooperative model, investigating how the "bundle of rights" inherent in a traditional membership can be disaggregated, or "unbundled," and selectively commodified into distinct financial instruments. Such instruments, if designed correctly, could attract new forms of capital and provide liquidity for stakeholders without violating the legal and philosophical pillars of the cooperative enterprise.

The analysis proceeds through three sequential and interlocking lenses: California Corporate Law, Federal Subchapter T Tax Law, and Federal Securities Law. Any proposed instrument must successfully navigate all three regulatory regimes to be viable. First, it must be permissible under the specific statutes governing cooperatives in California, which establish the fundamental guardrails for control, transferability, and distributions. Second, its design must ensure the entity continues "operating on a cooperative basis" to preserve the crucial single-taxation benefit of Subchapter T. Finally, if the instrument is structured in a way that constitutes a "security," it must comply with the registration and anti-fraud provisions of federal securities law. By systematically applying this three-lens analysis, this report provides a comprehensive guide for founders, advisors, and stakeholders to design innovative, compliant, and effective capital structures for the modern cooperative.

title.emphasis: Section I: The Foundation: The Bundle of Rights in a California Cooperative

section.para: To understand what can be commodified, one must first define the baseline: the specific bundle of ownership, management, and benefit rights established by the California Cooperative Corporation Law.3 The formation of a cooperative is not merely the creation of a new legal entity; it is a fundamental transformation of individual rights into a collective enterprise, governed by a unique set of statutory principles that prioritize the user-patron over the investor.

title.emphasis: A. The Primacy of the Patron-Member Relationship

section.para: The foundational principle of a California cooperative is its purpose. Unlike a standard corporation organized to maximize shareholder profit, a cooperative must be "organized and...conduct its business primarily for the mutual benefit of its members as patrons".6 The law explicitly states that cooperatives are "not organized to make a profit for themselves, as such, or for their members, as such, but primarily for their members as patrons".2 This statutory language establishes the primacy of the patron relationship. A member's core identity within the cooperative is that of a user of its services—a consumer buying goods, a worker providing labor, or a producer marketing a crop.1

All legitimate economic benefits flowing to members must derive from this relationship. The "earnings, savings, or benefits of the corporation" are to be used for the general welfare of the members or, more commonly, "proportionately and equitably distributed to some or all of its members or its patrons, based upon their patronage".6 This distribution, known as a patronage refund or dividend, can take the form of cash, property, or equity instruments like capital credits or memberships.2 The amount of the distribution is tied directly to the quantity or value of business the patron has conducted with the cooperative, not the amount of capital they have invested.7

title.emphasis: B. The Surrender and Transformation of Rights upon Formation

section.para: The act of forming a cooperative involves a deliberate subordination and transformation of rights that would otherwise be retained in a traditional business venture.

listitem.para: From Individual Profit to Collective Benefit: A founder or member extinguishes their individual right to seek profit from the enterprise's activities in the conventional sense. This right is transformed into a collective one: the right to receive a proportionate share of the cooperative's net margins, which are generated through the collective patronage of all members. The focus shifts from private accumulation to equitable distribution of collectively generated surplus.6

Encumbrance of Capital: Capital contributed to a cooperative, whether through membership fees or other investments, is fundamentally encumbered by the entity's cooperative purpose. It ceases to be purely private capital seeking the highest possible return and is instead subordinated to the operational needs of the patronage-focused enterprise.9 Its role is to serve as a tool to facilitate the cooperative's business with its patrons, not as an independent source of profit and control.

Transformation of Control: The quintessential right of capital in a standard corporation—control proportional to investment (i.e., one share, one vote)—is extinguished. It is replaced by a right to democratic control, where governance power is distributed among member-patrons, typically on an equal basis, irrespective of their individual capital contributions.1

title.emphasis: C. Statutory Guardrails (California Corporations Code)

section.para: The California Corporations Code establishes firm, non-negotiable guardrails that define the legal boundaries of a cooperative. Any attempt to unbundle and commodify rights must operate within these constraints.

listitem.para: Democratic Control: The cornerstone of the cooperative model is democratic governance. California Corporations Code § 12404 states unequivocally, "the voting power of members having voting rights shall be equal".10 This codifies the "one member, one vote" principle as the default and essential characteristic of a California cooperative.1 While the code allows for different classes of membership with varying proprietary rights, the voting power among those members who are granted a vote must be equal.12 The law does provide for narrow and specific exceptions, such as for voting in a "central organization" of cooperatives (a federated cooperative) under § 12314 or for the creation of non-voting "community investors" in worker cooperatives under § 12404.5.10 These exceptions, however, are tightly circumscribed and reinforce the general rule of equal voting power for patrons.

Transferability of Interests: In stark contrast to shares in a publicly-traded corporation, membership in a California cooperative is not, by default, a "saleable commodity".1 The Consumer Cooperative Corporation Law specifies that a membership is not transferable unless the cooperative's articles of incorporation or bylaws explicitly permit it.1 This default non-transferability serves as a critical protective mechanism, insulating the cooperative from speculative interests and ensuring that its ownership remains in the hands of active patrons who use its services. When transferability is permitted, the bylaws can and often do impose significant restrictions.14 For example, they may require that any potential transferee must meet certain qualifications, such as being actively engaged in a specific line of business or activity compatible with the cooperative's purpose.1 These restrictions are a powerful tool for maintaining the integrity of the membership base.
Distributions and the Subordination of Capital: The principle of subordinating capital is not merely a philosophical concept; it is enforced by statute.

The 15% Cap: California Corporations Code § 12451 imposes a hard ceiling on "distributions," which are defined to include dividends on capital but explicitly exclude patronage refunds.2 The law states that such distributions "in any fiscal year shall not exceed 15 percent, multiplied by contributions...to capital".15 This provision statutorily limits the return on capital, ensuring that the primary financial benefit of the cooperative flows through patronage, not investment.

Prohibited Distributions: The code further protects the cooperative's financial integrity and the primacy of the patronage system. Section 12453 prohibits a cooperative from making a patronage distribution to members out of earnings generated from non-member patronage.16 It also forbids any distribution (including redemptions of memberships) that would result in the cooperative being unable to meet its liabilities (i.e., would render it insolvent).16

section.para: The legal architecture of California cooperative law, while protective, contains the very tools needed for financial innovation. The statutes explicitly differentiate between voting power, which must generally remain equal among voting members, and proprietary interests (such as rights to distributions or assets upon liquidation), which can be unequal if the rules for determining them are set forth in the articles or bylaws.12 This statutory separation of control rights from financial rights is the fundamental legal gateway that permits the creation of non-voting equity instruments. A cooperative can, in compliance with the law, design a class of membership or stock that carries a right to a financial return (a proprietary interest) but carries no vote, thereby attracting passive capital without violating the sacrosanct principle of democratic control by patrons.

This framework, however, establishes a default state of maximum control and minimum liquidity. By defaulting to one-member-one-vote and non-transferability, the law creates a "closed-loop" system designed to preserve the cooperative's character and serve its active patrons.1 Any attempt to commodify rights and introduce transferability represents a deliberate move away from this protective default. Such a move is permissible but requires careful and intentional design, using the articles and bylaws to create new rules that still honor the spirit and letter of the law.

title.emphasis: Section II: The Federal Tax Filter: Preserving Subchapter T Status

section.para: Once an instrument is deemed permissible under California law, it must pass through the second, equally critical filter: federal tax law. The primary tax advantage of the cooperative structure is found in Subchapter T of the Internal Revenue Code (IRC §§ 1381-1388), which allows a cooperative to achieve single-taxation on its patronage-sourced earnings.9 By deducting patronage dividends paid to members, the cooperative avoids the double taxation faced by standard C-corporations, where profits are taxed first at the corporate level and again when distributed to shareholders.7 Losing this status would impose a significant financial burden and undermine a key rationale for choosing the cooperative form. This privileged tax treatment is not automatic; it is contingent upon the entity "operating on a cooperative basis," a standard defined not by a single statute but by a consistent body of judicial precedent and IRS interpretation.7

title.emphasis: A. The Three Pillars of "Operating on a Cooperative Basis"

section.para: To qualify for Subchapter T treatment, a cooperative must demonstrate adherence to three fundamental principles 9:

listitem.para: Subordination of Capital: This principle requires that capital serves the cooperative's operational purposes rather than being an end in itself. The return paid on capital must be limited, ensuring that the primary financial rewards of the enterprise flow from patronage, not investment. This aligns perfectly with California's 15% statutory cap on distributions and is a core test for the IRS.2 Structures like non-voting preferred stock with a fixed, limited dividend are designed specifically to meet this requirement by treating capital as a tool that receives a reasonable "rent" for its use, not a claim on all residual profits.20

Democratic Control: The cooperative must be controlled by its member-patrons on a democratic basis. The one-member, one-vote standard is the clearest and safest way to meet this test.8 While the IRS has shown some flexibility, any structure that cedes significant or controlling governance power to non-patron investors puts the cooperative's Subchapter T status at severe risk.21 Control must ultimately rest with those who use the cooperative's services.

Patronage-Based Operations: The cooperative's activities must be conducted for the benefit of its patrons, and the net earnings generated from that business must be allocated and distributed to them based on their respective patronage.9 This ensures that the cooperative exists to serve its users, not to accumulate profit for its own sake.

title.emphasis: B. The Critical Distinction: Patronage vs. Non-Patronage Sourced Income

section.para: The operational core of Subchapter T lies in the meticulous separation of income into two distinct "pockets": patronage-sourced and non-patronage-sourced.19 A non-exempt cooperative (the most common type) is permitted to deduct from its gross income the value of patronage dividends distributed to its members from patronage-sourced earnings.7 Conversely, any income derived from non-patronage sources is fully taxable at standard corporate rates, and only the after-tax remainder can be retained or distributed.9

listitem.para: Defining "Patronage-Sourced" Income: The IRC defines this as income "arising out of earnings of the cooperative from business done with or for its patrons".9 Federal regulations further clarify that this excludes earnings from business done with or for non-patrons, or from other sources unrelated to the core cooperative activity.22

The "Actually Facilitates" Test: The crucial test applied by the IRS and courts to determine the character of income is the "actually facilitates" test.23 For income to be considered patronage-sourced, the transaction that generated it must be "directly related to" and must "actually facilitate" the cooperative's primary business activities with its patrons.24 It cannot be merely incidental to the business or simply enhance overall profitability.22

Compliant Example: An agricultural marketing cooperative invests in and operates a grain elevator. The fees and margins earned from storing and handling its members' grain are patronage-sourced because the elevator directly facilitates the marketing of their products.23

Non-Compliant Example: A worker cooperative takes its retained earnings and invests them in a portfolio of publicly traded stocks. Any dividends or capital gains from this portfolio would be non-patronage-sourced income. The investment activity does not facilitate the work performed by the member-patrons; it is a separate, unrelated business venture.23

section.para: This dual-income system has profound implications for the design of any commodified financial instrument. If a cooperative wishes to issue a dividend-bearing instrument, such as preferred stock, to attract outside capital from non-patrons, the funds used to pay that dividend cannot be patronage-sourced earnings. To do so would violate the principle that patronage earnings must be returned to patrons. Therefore, for a non-exempt cooperative, the dividend on such stock must be paid from its "non-patronage" income pocket, which is subject to corporate income tax.9 This creates a significant tax drag; the cooperative must earn enough non-patronage revenue to pay corporate taxes and still have sufficient after-tax profit to pay the promised dividend. This makes capital raised through such instruments inherently more expensive than capital retained from patronage. The only exception is for "exempt" agricultural cooperatives under IRC §521, which have stricter operational requirements but are allowed a limited deduction for dividends paid on capital stock.25

The "actually facilitates" test, however, provides a powerful logic for designing innovative capital structures that remain within the patronage framework. The New Generation Cooperative (NGC) model is a prime example.27 In an NGC, a member makes a substantial upfront capital investment to acquire a "delivery right," which is both a right and an obligation to deliver a specific quantity of a commodity to the cooperative. While this upfront payment looks like a traditional investment, the capital raised is used to build a value-added processing facility (e.g., an ethanol plant) that the members will use. The return to the member comes not as a dividend on the initial investment, but in the form of higher prices or margins received for their processed product—a return generated directly by their patronage. The initial capital contribution is thus defensible as "actually facilitating" the cooperative's core patronage business, allowing the entire structure to be treated as a cohesive patronage-based system under Subchapter T.23 This model demonstrates that a large capital contribution can be integrated into the cooperative framework if its purpose is inextricably linked to enabling and enhancing the cooperative's primary business with its members.

title.emphasis: Section III: The Securities Law Gauntlet: When a Commodified Right Becomes a Security

section.para: After an instrument is cleared by California cooperative law and structured for Subchapter T compliance, it faces the final and often most complex hurdle: federal securities law. The moment a cooperative interest is designed to be transferable or to provide a financial return beyond the benefits of patronage, it risks being classified as a "security." Such a classification triggers a host of demanding obligations under the Securities Act of 1933 and the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, including potential registration with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and stringent anti-fraud liability. The analysis of whether a cooperative instrument is a security is a fact-intensive inquiry that hinges on its economic reality, not its name. A unified framework, integrating the key Supreme Court tests, is essential for this determination.23

title.emphasis: A. Initial Characterization: Debt or Equity?

section.para: The first analytical step is to characterize the fundamental nature of the instrument, as this determines which legal test to apply.23

listitem.para: Is it a "Note" or Debt-Like Instrument? This category includes instruments that involve a loan of money, a promise to repay a principal amount, and a stated interest rate (even if zero). If the instrument has these characteristics, the proper analysis falls under the Reves test.23

Is it an "Investment Contract" or Equity-Like Instrument? This category includes instruments representing a stake in the cooperative's assets or profits, where the holder's return is primarily dependent on the cooperative's financial success. This includes things like preferred stock, tradable delivery rights, or any instrument marketed for its potential appreciation. If so, the analysis proceeds under the Howey test.23

title.emphasis: B. Applying the Supreme Court Tests

listitem.para:
The
Reves
"Family Resemblance" Test for Notes: In Reves v. Ernst & Young, the Supreme Court established that every note is presumed to be a security. This presumption can be rebutted only if the note bears a strong "family resemblance" to a list of judicially created exceptions for notes with a commercial, rather than investment, character. The test involves analyzing four factors 23:

Motivations of Seller and Buyer: Is the cooperative raising money for general business use (leaning towards a security) or to facilitate a specific, short-term commercial transaction for a patron (leaning away)? Is the buyer primarily motivated by the potential for profit from the interest earned (leaning towards a security) or by a commercial purpose, such as financing a purchase from the cooperative (leaning away)?

Plan of Distribution: Is the note offered and traded on a public market or to a broad class of investors (leaning towards a security)? Or is its distribution narrowly limited to member-patrons for whom it facilitates a transaction (leaning away)?

Reasonable Expectations of the Investing Public: Is the instrument marketed and referred to as an "investment," a "bond," or a "note" in the investment sense (leaning towards a security)? Or is it consistently described as "patronage capital," a "member deposit," or an "advance" (leaning away)?

Presence of an Alternative Regulatory Scheme: Does the existing framework of state cooperative law and Subchapter T provide sufficient protection to the noteholder, making the application of federal securities law unnecessary (leaning away)?

The
Howey
Test for Investment Contracts: The seminal case of SEC v. W.J. Howey Co. defined an "investment contract" as a security if four conditions are met 23:
An Investment of Value: The holder must give up something of value, such as money, assets, or even significant labor, to acquire the instrument.
In a Common Enterprise: The holder's fortunes must be interwoven with and dependent upon the fortunes of the promoter (the cooperative) and other investors.

With an Expectation of Profit: The holder must be attracted to the instrument by the prospect of a valuable return on their investment. This "profit" can come in various forms, including capital appreciation, dividends, or other earnings derived from the enterprise's operations.

Derived Primarily from the Efforts of Others: The realization of the expected profit must depend predominantly on the managerial, entrepreneurial, or operational efforts of the cooperative's leadership or a third party.

section.para: A traditional, non-transferable membership in a consumer cooperative often evades security classification because it fails the "expectation of profit" prong of the Howey test. The member's primary motivation is not financial return but rather to gain access to goods or services at a lower cost or higher quality—a benefit of use, not of investment.1 However, the moment a cooperative unbundles rights and creates a special instrument designed to attract capital, the analysis shifts dramatically. An instrument that is transferable, can appreciate in value, or pays a dividend is almost certain to be classified as a security.
Consider the Class A Preferred Stock offered by Shared Capital Cooperative, a Subchapter T entity.20 Applying the
Howey test demonstrates why it is a security:

listitem.para: Investment of Value: Yes, investors purchase the shares for cash ($10 per share).
Common Enterprise: Yes, the ability to pay dividends depends entirely on the success of Shared Capital's lending operations, which are managed as a common enterprise.
Expectation of Profit: Yes, the instrument is explicitly marketed with a target annual dividend of 3-6%. This is a clear expectation of financial profit.

Efforts of Others: Yes, the generation of profits to pay the dividend depends entirely on the managerial efforts of Shared Capital's professional team. Because it squarely meets all four prongs, the Class A Preferred Stock is a security, which is precisely why Shared Capital undertook a formal offering process compliant with SEC regulations.

section.para: Furthermore, the argument that an "alternative regulatory scheme" obviates the need for securities law is a weak shield for commodified instruments sold to non-patrons. While state cooperative law provides robust protections for patron-members (e.g., democratic rights, access to information), these protections do not extend meaningfully to passive, non-patron investors. An outside investor in a non-voting instrument has no voice in governance and their primary interest—financial return—is statutorily subordinated to the interests of the patron-members.2 For example, the patron-controlled board could decide to forgo paying a preferred dividend in favor of making investments that lower costs for patrons, leaving the investor with no recourse under cooperative law. A court would likely recognize this vulnerability and conclude that the comprehensive disclosure and anti-fraud protections of federal securities law are essential to protect this class of stakeholder, rendering the "alternative regulatory scheme" defense ineffective.

title.emphasis: Section IV: Synthesis: A Framework for Designing Compliant Cooperative Instruments

section.para: Integrating the distinct requirements of California corporate law, federal Subchapter T, and securities law is the critical task in designing viable cooperative capital instruments. A failure in any one domain renders an instrument unworkable. This section presents a unified framework, operationalizing the analysis from the preceding sections into a practical tool for assessing the compliance and tradability of various potential instruments. The goal is to move from abstract principles to a concrete decision-making matrix.

title.emphasis: A. Defining the "Subchapter T Security"

section.para: The intersection of these legal regimes gives rise to a novel asset class that can be termed a "Subchapter T Security".23 This is an instrument that is legally classified as a security under the

Reves or Howey tests but is simultaneously and intentionally engineered to be fully compliant with the core principles of cooperative operation. It is a hybrid instrument. On one hand, it acknowledges the holder's expectation of a financial return, triggering securities law protections. On the other hand, it contractually and structurally subordinates that profit motive to the cooperative's primary patronage purpose. Its design must ensure that any value generated by the collective efforts of the cooperative's patrons is socialized for the benefit of all patrons, rather than being privatized and captured by the instrument holder alone. It is a tool for injecting capital into the cooperative commons, not for enclosing and extracting value from it.23

title.emphasis: B. The Three-Pillar Compliance Test

section.para: To qualify as a Subchapter T Security, any instrument must be tested against three pillars of cooperative compliance 23:

listitem.para: Subordination of Capital as Defense of the Commons: This pillar reframes the concept from a simple limit on returns to a fundamental test of purpose. The instrument's structure must be analyzed to determine if it is a tool for the private capture of cooperatively-created value or a defense against it. A compliant instrument is engineered to ensure that gains are captured by the cooperative and distributed according to patronage principles, thereby socializing the benefits of the capital rather than privatizing them.23

Democratic Member Control: The instrument must not dilute or undermine the democratic control of the patron-members. Any structure where an individual or entity can acquire disproportionate voting power in a single decision-making body by purchasing more securities is fundamentally non-compliant. This test permits models like a single voting security per member or multiple non-duplicative voting securities (e.g., one vote for the main board, a separate vote for a regional committee), but it forbids the standard corporate "one share, one vote" model.23

Patronage-Source Income Integrity: The capital raised by the instrument must pass the "actually facilitates" test. Its function must be inextricably linked to the cooperative's core mission and its business with its patrons. Instruments like per-unit capital retains or NGC delivery rights are compliant because the capital is used to enable and quantify patronage (e.g., by funding a processing plant). An instrument that raises capital for purposes unrelated to the cooperative's direct business with patrons, such as for a speculative investment fund, would sever this link and fail the test.23

title.emphasis: C. A Proposed Compliance and Tradability Matrix for Cooperative Instruments

section.para: The following matrix provides a comparative framework for assessing various potential instruments. It synthesizes the complex, multi-domain analysis into a single reference tool, allowing founders and advisors to visualize the strategic trade-offs inherent in different capitalization strategies. By showing how a design choice (e.g., adding a dividend or transferability) impacts compliance across all three legal regimes, it transforms abstract legal principles into a practical guide for structural decision-making.

row.entry: **Instrument Type**
**CA Corp. Law Compliance**
**Subchapter T Compliance**
**Securities Law Status (*****Reves/Howey*****)**
**Tradability Analysis & Risk**
**1. Standard Membership Share**
**High:** The baseline model. Equal voting power is the default (§12404).10 Default is non-transferable (§12404).1
**High:** Perfectly embodies the three pillars. The member's primary relationship is patronage.
**Low Risk of Security Classification:** Often fails the "expectation of profit" prong of the *Howey* test, as the benefit is cost savings or access to services, not financial return.1
**Non-Tradable:** Not considered a "saleable commodity".1 Liquidity is available only through redemption by the cooperative, subject to its bylaws and solvency.
**2. Non-Voting Preferred Stock (e.g., Shared Capital Model)**

**High:** Permitted by the statutory distinction between equal voting rights and potentially unequal proprietary rights (§12310).12 Dividends must adhere to the 15% annual cap on distributions (§12451).15

**Medium:** Compliant only if dividends are paid from the cooperative's after-tax non-patronage income.9 Risks failing the "subordination of capital" pillar if returns are excessive or if it creates pressure to prioritize non-patronage business over patronage business.

**High Likelihood of Security Classification:** Clearly meets all four prongs of the *Howey* test due to the investment of money for an expected profit (dividends) based on the efforts of management.20

**Privately Tradable:** Can be sold to investors, typically under a securities law exemption like Regulation D. Robust transfer restrictions in the bylaws (e.g., right of first refusal for the co-op) are essential to maintain control. Public trading is extremely high risk and likely incompatible with Subchapter T.
**3. Tradable Delivery Right (e.g., NGC Model)**
**High:** Can be structured as a contractual right tied to membership. The terms of transferability must be explicitly defined in the articles or bylaws.1

**High:** Passes the "actually facilitates" test, as the capital raised is directly used to enable and enhance the patronage activity (e.g., building a processing plant).23 The right's value is tied to the benefit of patronage, not passive profit.

**Medium Risk of Security Classification:** Likely meets the *Howey* test. There is an investment of value, a common enterprise, and the return depends on the efforts of management. The potential for the right to appreciate in value creates an expectation of profit.

**Internally Tradable:** Designed for trading within a closed pool of qualified patrons (e.g., other farmers who can meet the delivery obligation). A public market would sever the essential link to patronage and likely violate Subchapter T principles.
**4. Patronage-Linked Debt Note**
**High:** Cooperatives have the general power to borrow money and issue debt securities (§12320).3

**High:** Compliant if interest payments are treated as a legitimate cost of doing business that facilitates the cooperative's patronage activities (e.g., borrowing for operating capital to serve members).

**Medium Risk of Security Classification (under** ***Reves*****):** Highly fact-dependent. If marketed broadly to the public as an "investment note," it is almost certainly a security. If structured as a member loan program with limited distribution and framed as a commercial transaction, it may escape classification.23
**Privately Tradable (if a security):** Similar risk profile to preferred stock. If it is not a security, its tradability is governed purely by the terms of the contract.
**5. Special Management Share (Hypothetical)**
**Very Low:** Fundamentally violates the principle of equal voting power for members (§12404).10 It is unlikely to fit within the narrow statutory exceptions for worker or federated cooperatives.
**Very Low:** Directly violates the "democratic control" pillar of Subchapter T by concentrating power based on a special class of share rather than patronage.9
**High Likelihood of Security Classification:** A clear investment contract where the "profit" sought is control and influence over the enterprise.

**Not Recommended:** This structure is fundamentally incompatible with the legal and tax definitions of a cooperative. It represents a direct attempt to import the C-Corp governance model, which would disqualify the entity as a cooperative.

title.emphasis: Section V: Structural Models and Case Studies

section.para: To move from theoretical analysis to practical application, it is instructive to examine established and documented models that illustrate how these principles are applied in the real world. These cases demonstrate the spectrum of possibilities, from the most traditional structures to highly innovative capital-raising vehicles.

title.emphasis: A. The Traditional Model: The Indivisible Membership

section.para: The most common cooperative structure, particularly for consumer and worker cooperatives, is the traditional, indivisible membership. In this model, all rights—use, control, and benefit—are bundled into a single membership share or certificate.1 Capital is raised through one-time membership fees and, most significantly, through retained patronage refunds (also known as capital credits). In this system, a portion of the annual surplus allocated to a member is retained by the cooperative as equity, with the promise of redemption at a future date.29 The membership itself is typically non-transferable and can only be redeemed by the cooperative, often upon the member leaving the co-op. This structure maximizes the integrity of the "closed loop," ensuring ownership and control remain exclusively with active patrons. However, it offers minimal liquidity and can be slow to accumulate the large amounts of capital needed for major projects.

title.emphasis: B. The New Generation Cooperative (NGC) Model: Commodifying Patronage

section.para: The New Generation Cooperative, which emerged primarily in the agricultural sector, represents a major innovation in cooperative finance. The NGC commodifies the very right and obligation of patronage itself through the creation of tradable delivery rights.27 To join, a producer must make a significant upfront investment to purchase the right to deliver a specific quantity and quality of a commodity to the cooperative's value-added processing facility. This delivery right is a distinct, appreciable, and transferable asset.

This model brilliantly solves several problems. It raises substantial upfront capital to finance large-scale facilities. By making the delivery right an obligation as well as a right, it guarantees a stable supply of raw materials, allowing the plant to operate at peak efficiency.27 Crucially, it aligns with cooperative principles because the right is typically transferable only to other producers who can meet the delivery obligation, keeping ownership and control within the class of active patrons. The return on the investment comes from enhanced patronage—higher prices for the processed goods—not from a passive dividend. As analyzed previously, this structure is highly defensible under the "actually facilitates" test of Subchapter T.23

title.emphasis: C. The External Capital Model: The Shared Capital Cooperative Case Study

section.para: Shared Capital Cooperative, a loan fund that lends to other cooperatives, provides a well-documented example of a Subchapter T cooperative that successfully raised external capital from non-patrons. Its SEC filing details a capital structure that includes multiple classes of stock, most notably Class A Preferred Stock sold to outside investors.20 This instrument is a quintessential "Subchapter T Security."

listitem.para: Structure: The Class A Preferred Stock is non-voting, explicitly separating financial rights from control rights.

Return: It offers a discretionary, non-cumulative dividend with a target rate of 3-6%, clearly subordinating the return on capital to the financial health and mission of the cooperative. The board, controlled by borrowing members, is not obligated to pay a dividend in any given year.

Compliance: This structure respects the three pillars. Democratic control is preserved with the patron-borrowers. Subordination of capital is achieved through the discretionary and capped dividend. The capital raised "actually facilitates" the cooperative's mission of providing loans to its members.

Securities Law: Shared Capital acknowledged the Class A Preferred Stock is a security and undertook a formal offering process, providing full disclosure to investors about the nature of their investment and its subordinated status.20 It also includes strict transfer restrictions, requiring board approval and giving the cooperative a right of first refusal, thereby preventing a secondary market from developing outside its control.

title.emphasis: D. The Worker Co-op "Community Investor" Model

section.para: California law provides a unique statutory pathway for worker cooperatives to attract mission-aligned capital. California Corporations Code § 12253 and § 12404.5 create a special category for "community investors," defined as individuals or entities that invest in a worker cooperative but are not worker-members.13 This statute creates a specific, limited exception to the "one member, one vote" rule. It allows the articles or bylaws to grant these community investors voting rights, but their votes are subordinate to the worker-members. This model provides a legal framework for what is often called "multi-stakeholder" governance, allowing a cooperative to bring in capital from community allies who support its mission, while ensuring that ultimate control remains with the workers. This is a powerful, albeit narrowly targeted, tool for blending external capital with cooperative principles.

title.emphasis: Section VI: Strategic Recommendations and Risk Analysis

section.para: The preceding analysis demonstrates that while a California cooperative operates within a strict legal and tax framework, significant innovation in its capital structure is possible. The key is to select a pathway that aligns with the cooperative's strategic goals for capital, control, and liquidity, while meticulously adhering to the compliance requirements. The following recommendations outline three distinct strategic pathways and conclude with a critical risk mitigation checklist.

title.emphasis: A. Pathway to Commodified, Non-Tradable Rights

section.para: This pathway is ideal for cooperatives that wish to raise additional capital from their existing membership base or provide a return to members who contribute more capital, without creating an external market for their equity.

listitem.para: Structure: The primary instruments are non-transferable preferred membership shares or patronage advance certificates. These instruments would be issued only to members and would carry a preferred, but limited and likely discretionary, dividend.
Purpose: To create an internal mechanism for members to invest further in their cooperative and receive a modest, non-patronage-based return on that extra investment.

Implementation: The rights and limitations of these instruments must be clearly defined in the cooperative's articles and bylaws. The dividend must comply with California's 15% cap on distributions (§ 12451).15 Liquidity is provided exclusively through redemption by the cooperative, which is itself subject to the solvency requirements of § 12453, meaning the cooperative cannot redeem shares if doing so would jeopardize its financial stability.16 This structure is low-risk from a securities perspective as there is no public plan of distribution.

title.emphasis: B. Pathway to Commodified, Privately-Traded Rights

section.para: This pathway is designed for cooperatives that need to raise significant capital from outside, mission-aligned investors, including non-patrons. It requires a sophisticated understanding of securities law.

listitem.para: Structure: The instruments of choice are non-voting preferred stock (like the Shared Capital model) or, in an agricultural context, tradable delivery rights (like the NGC model).20 These instruments are intentionally designed as securities.

Process: The cooperative must acknowledge that it is offering a security and comply with federal and state securities laws. This typically involves structuring the offering to qualify for an exemption from the full, costly registration process. Common exemptions include Regulation D private placements, which limit the sale to "accredited investors," or intrastate offering exemptions.

Implementation: This is the most critical element. The cooperative must draft and enforce robust transfer restrictions within its bylaws and the investor subscription agreements. A right of first refusal, giving the cooperative the option to buy back the shares before they can be sold to a third party, is an essential tool for maintaining control over the ownership base. Full and transparent disclosure to potential investors is paramount; they must understand the subordinated nature of their investment, the discretionary nature of their returns, and the severe limitations on liquidity.

title.emphasis: C. The High Bar for Publicly-Traded Rights

section.para: This pathway, which involves creating a cooperative equity instrument that can be freely bought and sold by the general public on a stock exchange, is fundamentally incompatible with the cooperative model and is not recommended.

listitem.para: Analysis: A publicly-traded security would fail the core compliance tests. The profit-maximization motive of public market investors is diametrically opposed to the principle of subordination of capital.9 A public market would create immense pressure to prioritize shareholder returns over patron benefits, violating the essence of Subchapter T. It would destroy democratic control, as ownership would shift to a dispersed and anonymous group of non-patrons with no connection to the cooperative's mission. The "closed loop" of patronage that cooperative law is designed to protect would be irrevocably broken.1 From a securities law perspective, under the Reves and Howey tests, the instrument would be so clearly an investment for profit that it would be difficult to argue it retains any cooperative character at all.23

Conclusion: Attempting to create a publicly-traded cooperative security is an attempt to merge two irreconcilable legal and economic structures. The result would almost certainly be the loss of Subchapter T tax status and a legal challenge to its status as a cooperative under California law.

title.emphasis: D. Risk Mitigation Checklist for Founders

section.para: When drafting the foundational documents for a cooperative contemplating an innovative capital structure, the following points must be meticulously addressed in the Articles of Incorporation and Bylaws:

listitem.para: [ ] Purpose Clause: State clearly that the corporation is a cooperative organized under the Cooperative Corporation Law and that its primary purpose is the mutual benefit of its members as patrons.3
[ ] Membership Qualifications: Define precisely who is eligible for membership, tying eligibility directly to patronage of the cooperative's services.1
[ ] Voting Rights: Explicitly state that voting rights for members are equal, adhering to the one-member, one-vote principle (§ 12404), unless utilizing a specific statutory exception.10

[ ] Classes of Equity: If creating multiple classes of equity (e.g., voting common and non-voting preferred), define the rights, preferences, privileges, and restrictions of each class with absolute clarity, especially the absence of voting rights for any non-patron class.12

[ ] Transfer Restrictions: State that memberships and other equity are not transferable except as provided in the bylaws. The bylaws should then detail the restrictions, including, at a minimum, a right of first refusal for the cooperative.1

[ ] Dividend Policy: For any dividend-bearing instrument, state that dividends are discretionary, non-cumulative, and subject to the 15% cap under § 12451. Specify that they will be paid from non-patronage sources (for non-exempt co-ops).9
[ ] Redemption Rights: Clarify that any redemption of shares is at the sole discretion of the board and is subject to the solvency requirements of § 12453.16
[ ] Patronage Definition: Define what constitutes "patronage" and the formula for calculating and distributing patronage refunds to ensure compliance with Subchapter T.6

[ ] Investor Disclosures: For any instrument sold to outsiders, create a comprehensive disclosure document (e.g., a private placement memorandum) that clearly and forcefully explains the risks, the subordinated nature of the investment, the lack of control, and the severe limits on transferability and liquidity.

title.emphasis: Conclusion: The Future of Cooperative Capitalization

section.para: The analysis reveals that the California cooperative, governed by state corporate law and federal Subchapter T, is a resilient and surprisingly flexible legal structure. While its foundational principles of democratic control and subordination of capital create inherent friction with conventional capital markets, they do not foreclose the possibility of financial innovation. The legal framework permits the careful unbundling of ownership rights, allowing for the creation of distinct, non-voting equity instruments that can attract capital without fatally compromising the cooperative's identity.

The viability of any such instrument hinges on its purpose. The most successful and legally defensible structures—from the tradable delivery rights of New Generation Cooperatives to the non-voting preferred stock of mission-driven loan funds—are those designed to use capital as a tool to defend and enhance the cooperative commons. They raise funds to facilitate and deepen the cooperative's core patronage business, ensuring that the ultimate benefits of the capital are socialized and returned to the patrons who create the value.23

Conversely, any instrument designed primarily to extract value from the cooperative for the private benefit of an investor is destined to fail the compliance tests. Structures that attempt to import the governance model of a standard corporation or promise market-rate, non-subordinated returns will inevitably violate the legal pillars of democratic control and subordination of capital. The pathway to a publicly-traded cooperative security remains, for these reasons, legally and philosophically untenable for an entity wishing to retain its cooperative status.

Ultimately, a cooperative can adapt to the demands of the modern economy and raise substantial capital. It can create special classes of ownership and even instruments that can be privately traded. However, it cannot do so by abandoning its core principles. It must innovate from within its own unique logic, using its legal and financial tools not to mimic the investor-owned corporation, but to strengthen its capacity to serve its member-patrons.
